title: Educational gradient in migration trajectories of young adult Indonesians
abstract: ABSTRACT During young adulthood, individuals experience various life-course transitions that can both drive migration and be influenced by it. Educational attainment is one of the key factors that explains variations in migration trajectories across population groups. However, the interplay between migration trajectories and education has received limited empirical focus in many countries, including Indonesia. This study explores the association between education and migration trajectories during young adulthood in Indonesia, utilising life-course trajectory data from all waves of the Indonesian Family Life Survey. This study reveals that approximately 40 per cent of Indonesians migrate internally during young adulthood, with a notable proportion undertaking additional migrations. Tertiary-educated individuals exhibit the highest youth migration rates, but primary and lower secondary groups initiate their migration trajectories slightly earlier. Furthermore, higher educational attainment correlates with an increased propensity for subsequent migration to new regions, whereas individuals with the lowest education levels are more likely to settle permanently in their initial destination. Post-migration education is also significantly associated with repeat migration. Examining the educational gradient in migration trajectories during young adulthood provides a more nuanced understanding of migration dynamics from a life-course perspective.
